symptoms.symptom_name: Inflammatory back pain pattern — prolonged early-morning stiffness improving with movement, insidious onset under age 45

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Morning stiffness >30 min easing with activity and worsening with rest is the hallmark inflammatory back-pain pattern (ASAS).
Mechanical pain typically worsens with activity and eases with rest — the opposite pattern.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bilateral heel pain at Achilles insertion (enthesitis, patient-reported)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Enthesitis, classically at the Achilles, is a defining peripheral feature of spondyloarthritis.
Objective enthesitis on palpation supports spondyloarthritis; requires in-person examination.
Fatigue is a prominent constitutional feature of active inflammatory disease, though non-specific in isolation.
Fatigue does not discriminate mechanical strain from other causes.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bilateral sacroiliac joint tenderness on palpation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sacroiliac tenderness localises inflammation to the sacroiliac joints, the axis of axial spondyloarthritis; requires examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reduced lumbar flexion (positive modified Schober test)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Restricted spinal mobility on the modified Schober test reflects axial involvement; requires examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intact bladder/bowel control and normal perianal (saddle) sensation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of sphincter dysfunction and saddle anaesthesia on direct history is the key patient-reportable feature that makes cauda equina very unlikely and safely avoids emergency escalation.

symptoms.symptom_name: No radicular leg pain and no lower-limb weakness on history

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of radicular pain and leg weakness argues against nerve-root compression, though definitive exclusion needs an in-person neurological examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal straight leg raise and intact lower-limb reflexes

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal SLR and reflexes further exclude radiculopathy; requires in-person examination the AI Doctor cannot perform.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile, no unexplained weight loss, no night sweats

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of systemic constitutional features lowers the probability of a sinister infective/malignant cause; imaging still needed for definitive exclusion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe pain unresponsive to paracetamol with functional immobility

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Severity and poor response to simple analgesia reflect an active inflammatory flare and drive the need for prescription-strength anti-inflammatory therapy.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: No single feature is diagnostic, but inflammatory back-pain pattern + bilateral Achilles enthesitis + prominent fatigue with insidious onset under 45 together form a high-probability axial spondyloarthritis constellation that warrants rheumatology referral and MRI/HLA-B27 workup.

The combination of negative cauda equina, radicular and systemic screens is what safely keeps an alarming, immobilising back-pain presentation in the outpatient tier — the pattern the case tests the AI Doctor's ability to recognise rather than reflexively over-escalate.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Detects active sacroiliitis and is the key investigation to confirm non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis; arranged via GP/rheumatology, not an emergency scan.
Supports classification of axial spondyloarthritis in the appropriate clinical context; arranged via routine GP bloods.
Inflammatory markers help gauge disease activity and, if markedly raised with any systemic features, would raise the threshold for reconsidering infection.
Not indicated acutely for low back pain without red flags and insensitive for early sacroiliitis; MRI is the appropriate imaging — ordering an urgent X-ray here is over-investigation.
